title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a scientist called Sarah Thorne talking about the growth of hydroponic farming in urban areas. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a scientist called Sarah Thorne talking about the growth of hydroponic farming in urban areas. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Sarah Thorne explains how soil-free farming is changing the way cities produce food.
extracts.audio_script: Hello everyone. My name is Sarah Thorne and I’m a specialist in urban agriculture. Today I want to talk about a revolution happening right under our noses—or rather, inside abandoned warehouses. Hydroponics, the method of growing plants without soil, is transforming cities. [7]I first became convinced of its potential after seeing a project that used vertical hydroponic towers[/7] to grow lettuce in a basement in London. It was incredible to see such productivity in a dark, cramped space. In a hydroponic system, plants get their nutrients directly from water. [8]We use a carefully balanced nutrient solution to ensure the plants grow faster than they would in a field[/8]. Because we control the environment, we don't need pesticides. However, the system is sensitive. [9]I found that monitoring the health of the strawberry crops was the most time-consuming task[/9], as they are particularly susceptible to slight changes in pH levels. One of the biggest advantages is water efficiency; we use 90% less water than traditional farming. But there are costs. [10]The expense of running the air conditioning to maintain a stable temperature is a major factor for many startups[/10]. It can make or break a business. In our project, we also focus on the social side. [11]We’ve had great success using community volunteers to help with the daily maintenance of the systems[/11]. It brings people together. Last summer, we held an event to showcase our work. [12]The atmosphere at our first harvest festival was truly electric[/12]; seeing people eat food grown just a few meters away was wonderful. I believe this technology is vital for our future. [13]I am certain that food security is the most pressing issue that hydroponics can help solve[/13] as our population grows. As we like to say at the lab, [14]we are not just gardening, we are 'growing up' to meet the challenges of the next century[/14]. It’s an exciting field to be in.

dialogue.speaker: sarah
dialogue.text: Hello everyone. My name is Sarah Thorne and I’m a specialist in urban agriculture. Today I want to talk about a revolution happening right under our noses—or rather, inside abandoned warehouses. Hydroponics, the method of growing plants without soil, is transforming cities. I first became convinced of its potential after seeing a project that used vertical hydroponic towers to grow lettuce in a basement in London. It was incredible to see such productivity in a dark, cramped space. In a hydroponic system, plants get their nutrients directly from water. We use a carefully balanced nutrient solution to ensure the plants grow faster than they would in a field. Because we control the environment, we don't need pesticides. However, the system is sensitive. I found that monitoring the health of the strawberry crops was the most time-consuming task, as they are particularly susceptible to slight changes in pH levels. One of the biggest advantages is water efficiency; we use 90% less water than traditional farming. But there are costs. The expense of running the air conditioning to maintain a stable temperature is a major factor for many startups. It can make or break a business. In our project, we also focus on the social side. We’ve had great success using community volunteers to help with the daily maintenance of the systems. It brings people together. Last summer, we held an event to showcase our work. The atmosphere at our first harvest festival was truly electric; seeing people eat food grown just a few meters away was wonderful. I believe this technology is vital for our future. I am certain that food security is the most pressing issue that hydroponics can help solve as our population grows. As we like to say at the lab, we are not just gardening, we are 'growing up' to meet the challenges of the next century. It’s an exciting field to be in.

questions.question: Sarah’s interest in hydroponics was sparked by a visit to a site using (7) in a city basement.

questions.explanation: Monitoring the health of the strawberry crops was the most time-consuming task.

questions.question: The high cost of (10) can be a significant financial problem for new hydroponic companies.
questions.explanation: The expense of running the air conditioning is a major factor for startups.

questions.question: Sarah was pleased that (11) were willing to participate in the project's upkeep.
questions.explanation: They had success using community volunteers for daily maintenance.

questions.question: Sarah uses the word (12) to describe the mood during the project's first public event.
questions.explanation: She says the atmosphere at the first harvest festival was 'truly electric'.

questions.question: Sarah believes that the primary benefit of soil-free farming is improving (13) .
questions.explanation: She is certain that food security is the most pressing issue it can help solve.

questions.question: The team uses the expression (14) as a way to describe their innovative approach to farming.
questions.explanation: She says they are 'growing up' to meet future challenges.
